title: Dog Sneezing Fits: Causes, Triage, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog having occasional sneezing fits? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to triage your pet's symptoms at home.
content_html:
TL;DR: Most sneezing fits in dogs are normal reactions to irritants, but persistent sneezing or discharge requires a veterinary exam. Providing a video of the event is highly helpful for distinguishing between normal sneezes, reverse sneezes, and coughing.

What exactly is a sneezing fit in dogs?

An occasional sneezing fit in dogs is typically a rapid-fire succession of sneezes used to clear the nasal passages. This is often a normal physiological response to environmental irritants like dust, pollen, or strong fragrances. Some dogs also engage in 'play sneezing' when they are excited or interacting with other dogs, which is a harmless form of communication.

How do I know if my dog's sneezing fit is a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. Most occasional sneezing fits are not a medical emergency.
  • When to monitor at home: If your dog is acting like their usual self, maintaining a good appetite, and the sneezing stops quickly.
  • When to call the vet: If the sneezing becomes constant, is accompanied by thick discharge, or if your dog is pawing at their face in distress, you should schedule a non-emergency appointment.

How does sharing a photo or video of the sneezing fit help the triage process?

  • Identify the type of sneeze: A video allows veterinary professionals to distinguish between a regular sneeze, a "reverse sneeze" (which sounds more like a honk), or a respiratory cough.
  • Determine the likely cause: Seeing the dog's posture and the frequency of the fits helps determine if the cause is an irritant, an allergy, or potentially a foreign object stuck in the nasal passage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute rhinitis in dogs commonly manifests as sneezing, nasal discharge (initially serous, becoming mucoid or mucopurulent with secondary bacterial infection or inflammatory cell infiltration), pawing at the face, respiratory stertor, open-mouth breathing, and/or inspiratory dyspnea. Sneezing is an attempt to clear the upper airways and is most frequent in acute cases. Reverse sneezing, a paroxysmal episode of inspiratory effort to clear the nasopharynx, may also occur. Nasal mite (Pneumonyssoides caninum) infestation can cause sneezing, epistaxis, reverse sneezing, impaired scenting ability, facial pruritus, nasal discharge, head shaking, and stridor. Differential diagnoses for sneezing include rhinitis (idiopathic, secondary bacterial, parasitic, or fungal), oronasal neoplasia, dental disease (oronasal fistula), nasal foreign body, or nasopharyngeal disease (foreign body or mass lesions).

Chapter: Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: Can allergies cause my dog to sneeze?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs can be sensitive to environmental allergens such as pollen, mold, and dust, which can trigger occasional sneezing fits.

faqs.question: What is a reverse sneeze?
faqs.answer: A reverse sneeze occurs when air is pulled rapidly inward through the nose, often sounding like a loud snort. While it looks scary, it is usually harmless.

faqs.question: When does sneezing require a vet visit?
faqs.answer: You should contact a vet if you see blood in the mucus, if the sneezing is persistent for more than 24 hours, or if your dog's eye is squinting on the same side as the sneezing.
